nodes.text:
Red flags?
(hemoptysis, weight loss,
voice change, dysphagia,
smoker >45 y)

Specialist:
HRCT, bronchoscopy,
cough reflex test,
pH monitoring

items.text: Cough duration: acute (<3 wk), subacute (3–8 wk), chronic (>8 wk)
Cough character: dry vs. productive, timing (night/morning/day), position-related
Red flag check: hemoptysis, weight loss >5%, voice change >3 wk, dysphagia, smoker >45 y, dyspnea at rest
Medication list: ACE inhibitor? (most common iatrogenic cause — switch to ARB)
Smoking / vaping / passive smoke exposure
Environmental exposures: work (dust, chemicals), home (mold, pets), hobbies
Associated symptoms: nasal congestion/drip, heartburn, wheeze, throat clearing, voice fatigue
Impact on quality of life: sleep disturbance, social embarrassment, urinary incontinence
Chest X-ray (mandatory in chronic cough workup)
Spirometry + bronchodilator test
CBC + CRP (infection, eosinophilia)
FeNO: >25 ppb suggests eosinophilic inflammation → predicts ICS response
Consider PEF monitoring 2 weeks if cough-variant asthma suspected
Step 1: Nasal corticosteroid (mometasone / fluticasone furoate) 2–4 weeks → Response: upper airway cough syndrome (post-nasal drip)
Step 2: ICS (budesonide 400–800 µg/day) 6–8 weeks → Response: eosinophilic inflammation / cough-variant asthma
Step 3: PPI (omeprazole/esomeprazole 20–40 mg ×2) 8 weeks → Response: reflux cough (note: evidence weaker; best for acid reflux, less for non-acid)
Step 4: No response to any → Refractory chronic cough; specialist referral (pulmonologist / ENT)
Assess response at each step: use cough VAS or Leicester Cough Questionnaire (LCQ) before and after trial
HRCT thorax (bronchiectasis, ILD, small airway disease)
Bronchoscopy + BAL (if indicated: suspected malignancy, ILD, infection)
Cough reflex sensitivity test (capsaicin provocation — identifies cough hypersensitivity)
24h pH-impedance monitoring (acid + non-acid reflux events)
Laryngoscopy (EILO, LPR — laryngopharyngeal reflux)
Methacholine challenge (if cough-variant asthma suspected but spirometry normal)
Consider cough hypersensitivity syndrome: central sensitization of cough reflex; triggers at normal stimuli levels
Neuromodulator therapy: gabapentin 300→1800 mg/day (NNT ~4); trial 4–8 weeks; taper if effective
Alternative: pregabalin 75–300 mg/day or low-dose morphine 5–10 mg ×2/day (specialist-led)
Speech and language therapy (SLT): cough suppression techniques, laryngeal hygiene, breathing retraining; evidence-based
Gefapixant (P2X3 antagonist): EMA-approved for refractory chronic cough; dysgeusia is common side effect
Multimodal approach recommended: combine neuromodulator + SLT for best outcomes
